title: SEGMENT 17 — Pulling it together: the four ways bacteria fight back

lines.text: SEGMENT 17 — Pulling it together: the four ways bacteria fight back
We've now met every major target, and along the way I kept dropping in resistance mechanisms — beta-lactamases, PBP2a, D-Ala-D-Lac, erm methylation, rpoB mutations.

Let me now gather them into a single framework, because here's the beautiful thing: there are really only four fundamental strategies a bacterium can use to defeat any antibiotic, and once you see them as a set, every specific example we discussed slots neatly into one of the four.
This is the synthesis I want you to walk out with.
Strategy one: destroy or modify the drug.
If you can chemically wreck the molecule before it reaches its target, you win.
The supreme example is the beta-lactamase enzyme, which cleaves the beta-lactam ring and renders the drug inert.
There are also aminoglycoside-modifying enzymes that bolt chemical groups onto aminoglycosides so they can no longer bind the ribosome.
The drug arrives, the bacterium chemically disarms it.
Enzymatic inactivation.
Strategy two: modify the target.
Change the lock so the key no longer fits, while keeping the lock functional enough to do its job.
We saw this one again and again — it might be the most important strategy of all.
PBP2a in MRSA: a modified transpeptidase the beta-lactam can't bind.
The D-Ala-D-Lac swap in VRE: a modified cell-wall terminus vancomycin can't grip.
The erm methylation of ribosomal RNA: a modified ribosome that defeats macrolides, clindamycin, and streptogramins all at once.
The rpoB point mutation: a modified RNA polymerase rifampin can't hold.
In every case, the drug arrives perfectly intact — but the target has changed shape, and the binding fails.
Target modification.
Strategy three: stop the drug from getting in.
If the drug can never reach its target, the target's vulnerability is irrelevant.

Gram-negative bacteria are masters of this because of that outer membrane — they can reduce the number of porin channels, narrowing the doors through which drugs enter, and just like that the intracellular drug concentration drops below what's needed.
This is a major mechanism of resistance to beta-lactams and fluoroquinolones in gram-negatives.
Reduced permeability — locking the door.
Strategy four: pump the drug back out.

Even if the drug gets in, the bacterium can install molecular pumps in its membrane — efflux pumps — that grab the antibiotic and actively eject it, keeping the internal concentration too low to be effective.
Some efflux pumps are specific to one drug; others are alarmingly promiscuous and pump out many different antibiotic classes, conferring multi-drug resistance from a single mechanism.
This is important for tetracyclines, fluoroquinolones, macrolides, and more.
Active efflux — bailing out the boat as fast as the water comes in.
Four strategies.
Destroy the drug, change the target, keep it out, pump it out.
Now here's the unifying insight I most want to leave you with.
Look back across the entire lecture and you'll notice that for every drug, the resistance mechanism is logically predictable from the mechanism of action.
A drug that depends on binding a specific enzyme can be defeated by mutating that enzyme.
A drug with a reactive chemical ring invites an enzyme that cleaves the ring.
A large drug that struggles to enter can be locked out further.
A drug that must accumulate inside can be pumped out.
If you understand how a drug works, you can predict — before you ever see it in a clinic — how bacteria will likely escape it.
Mechanism predicts resistance.
That is the deepest payoff of approaching this subject through mechanism rather than memorization.
And one more layer, because it matters for how you'll practice or do research: where do these resistance genes come from, and why are they spreading so fast?

Bacteria don't only inherit resistance from their parents — they trade resistance genes horizontally, swapping mobile chunks of DNA called plasmids directly between cells, even between different species.
A resistance gene that arises in one bug can hop to another overnight.

Layer on top of that the relentless selection pressure we apply by using antibiotics — every dose kills the susceptible bacteria and leaves the resistant ones to flourish and multiply — and you have a recipe for the resistance crisis that brought colistin back from retirement.
We are, in a real sense, the evolutionary force selecting for our own obsolescence every time we use these drugs carelessly.
